jokes.text: Hymns here! I got hymns here! Get 'em while they're holy! Fresh from God's brain to your mouth!
jokes.punchline: Treating hymns like hot dogs with over-the-top sales pitch
jokes.explanation: Clever subversion of both street vendor calls and religious reverence. Economical and character-appropriate for Bart's irreverence.

jokes.text: And now please rise for our opening hymn... uh, 'In the Garden of Eden'... by I. Ron Butterfly.
jokes.punchline: Iron Butterfly disguised as 'I. Ron Butterfly' to sneak rock music into church
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay that sets up the entire episode's premise. The slight mispronunciation makes it believable that Lovejoy would fall for it.

jokes.text: Hey, Marge, remember when we used to make out to this hymn?
jokes.setup: Romantic nostalgia moment during church service
jokes.punchline: Homer being inappropriately nostalgic about making out to what he thinks is a hymn
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer character moment - oblivious and inappropriate. Shows he doesn't realize it's rock music.

jokes.text: Wait a minute. This sounds like rock and/or roll.
jokes.setup: Gradual realization something is wrong with the 'hymn'
jokes.punchline: Formal, stilted way of saying 'rock and roll'
jokes.explanation: The 'and/or' construction is perfectly pompous for Lovejoy's character while being genuinely funny. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: where I will eat naught but burning-hot coals... and drink naught but burning-hot cola
jokes.setup: Dramatic hell description with archaic language
jokes.punchline: Coal/cola wordplay in the middle of fire and brimstone
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay that maintains the biblical tone while being absurd. The alliteration makes it even funnier.

jokes.text: and strewn upon a parade of murderers and single mothers
jokes.punchline: Equating single mothers with murderers in casual list
jokes.explanation: Dark social satire disguised as religious fervor. Perfect character moment showing Lovejoy's conservative judgment.

jokes.text: Bart did it! - That Bart right there! - Milhouse!
jokes.punchline: Immediate betrayal followed by Bart's disappointed response
jokes.explanation: Classic Milhouse cowardice moment. The quick betrayal is predictable but executed well.

jokes.text: It's just something they made up to scare kids... like the boogeyman, or Michael Jackson.
jokes.setup: Dismissing the concept of souls as made-up
jokes.punchline: Equating Michael Jackson with mythical scary figures
jokes.explanation: Topical reference that captures mid-90s cultural moment. Slightly cheap celebrity joke but well-placed.

jokes.text: Why would they lie? What would they have to gain?
jokes.punchline: Complete obliviousness to obvious motivations
jokes.explanation: Perfect Milhouse innocence contrasting with obvious reality. Character-driven irony.

jokes.text: Oh, it can swim. It's even got wheels, in case you die in the desert and it has to drive to the cemetery.
jokes.punchline: Increasingly ridiculous soul transportation methods
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist escalation. The specificity of 'drive to the cemetery' makes it genuinely surreal and funny.

jokes.text: How can someone with glasses that thick be so stupid?
jokes.punchline: Linking thick glasses to intelligence ironically
jokes.explanation: Somewhat predictable insult playing on nerd stereotypes, but fits Bart's character.

jokes.text: Professor V.J. Cornucopia's Fantastic Foodmagorium and Great American Steakery.
jokes.punchline: Ridiculously over-the-top restaurant name
jokes.explanation: Satirizes chain restaurant naming conventions perfectly. Length is part of the joke but hurts economy score.

jokes.text: Aah! Natural light! Get it off me! Get it off me!
jokes.punchline: Moe reacting to sunlight like a vampire
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment establishing Moe's seediness. The vampire-like reaction is unexpected and character-specific.

jokes.text: Daddy, this place... smells like tinkle!
jokes.punchline: Blunt assessment of bar's condition
jokes.explanation: Child's honesty cutting through adult politeness. Effective but predictable.

jokes.text: Ah, but, Moe, the dank- the dank!
jokes.setup: Moe considering removing the bar's dank atmosphere
jokes.punchline: Barney's passionate defense of dankness itself
jokes.explanation: Barney treating 'dank' as a precious commodity is perfectly absurd and character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Aaah! No! It's dripping funny-smelling water all over me!
jokes.punchline: Present is actually a gross dead fish
jokes.explanation: Standard misdirection gag. Works but not particularly inventive.

jokes.text: Well, whether or not the soul is physically real, Bart... it's the symbol of everything fine inside us.
jokes.setup: Bart dismissing soul belief as junk
jokes.punchline: Lisa's earnest philosophical response to Bart's cynicism
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa character moment - intellectual and earnest. More plot setup than pure comedy.

jokes.text: Well, if you think he got such a good deal, I'll sell you my conscience for 4.50. I'll throw in my sense of decency too. It's a Bart sales event! Everything about me must go!
jokes.setup: Lisa's concern about the soul sale
jokes.punchline: Bart treating his moral qualities like clearance merchandise
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation that perfectly captures Bart's character and satirizes consumer culture. 'Sales event' language applied to morality is genius.

jokes.text: Geez! You're pretty uppity for someone who eats bugs all day.
jokes.setup: Santa's Little Helper growling at Bart
jokes.punchline: Bart insulting the dog for being judgmental
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bart's defensiveness, though the insult is somewhat generic.

jokes.text: Thank you, door!
jokes.setup: Automatic door not working for Bart
jokes.punchline: Thanking an inanimate object sarcastically
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective moment of frustration. More plot advancement than strong comedy.

jokes.text: Way to breathe, No-Breath.
jokes.setup: Bart trying to fog mirror with breath
jokes.punchline: Insulting his own reflection for not breathing
jokes.explanation: Clever nickname creation in the moment of frustration. Shows Bart's personality while advancing the soul-loss plot.

jokes.text: How about Chairman Moe's Magic Wok? I like it! Nah. I want something that says people can have a nice, relaxing time. I got it! Madman Moe's Pressure Cooker!
jokes.punchline: Suggesting increasingly threatening names while claiming they sound relaxing
jokes.explanation: Perfect character comedy showing Moe's complete lack of self-awareness. The contradiction is beautifully absurd.

jokes.text: You could flash-fry a buffalo in 40 seconds. Forty seconds? But I want it now!
jokes.punchline: Homer's impatience even with absurdly fast cooking time
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - his immediate gratification desire overrides any sense of reasonable time. Good character-specific comedy.

jokes.text: I know that's funny, but I'm just not laughing.
jokes.punchline: Acknowledging he should find it funny but doesn't
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-moment where Bart becomes conscious of his lack of response. Perfect economy and demonstrates the soul loss effect.

jokes.text: Pablo Neruda said laughter is the language of the soul.
jokes.setup: Bart's inability to laugh at cartoons
jokes.punchline: Lisa citing high poetry to explain Bart's condition
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa character moment - turning to literature to understand the situation. Sets up the soul test beautifully.

jokes.text: Cover me, Sarge. I'm going after Bart's soul.
jokes.punchline: Incorporating Bart's soul into military play scenario
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist moment where child's play incorporates the episode's central concept. Perfect Milhouse character beat.

jokes.text: Now, that's 'Moe' like it!
jokes.punchline: Terrible pun on 'more like it' using his own name
jokes.explanation: Groan-worthy pun that's perfectly in character for Moe's attempt at catchiness. Cheap but effective character moment.

jokes.text: Uh, no old people. They're not covered by our insurance.
jokes.punchline: Casual exclusion of elderly customers for insurance reasons
jokes.explanation: Dark, unexpected turn in the middle of upbeat commercial. Perfect economy and character-specific cynicism.

jokes.text: Hmm. Bart, what's wrong? There's something a little off about your hug.
jokes.punchline: Motherly intuition detecting something wrong with the hug
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge moment - maternal awareness of subtle changes. Advances soul-loss plot through character.

jokes.text: My soul's my best friend! My soul's like a toy that never breaks.
jokes.punchline: Innocent descriptions contrasting with Bart's situation
jokes.explanation: Good ironic counterpoint to Bart's situation. Child-like simplicity makes his predicament more poignant.

jokes.text: Bart sold his soul And that's just swell Now he's going straight to Hell
jokes.punchline: Cheerfully singing about Bart's damnation
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark turn in innocent sing-song. The cheerful tone contrasting with damnation content is perfectly executed.

jokes.text: An alligator with sunglasses? Huh! Now I've seen everything.
jokes.punchline: Casual reaction to absurd sight
jokes.explanation: Standard Simpsons background absurdity joke. Homer's blasé reaction sells it.

jokes.text: Please take the fries off my head, kid. The basket is extremely hot.
jokes.punchline: Breaking character to address physical discomfort
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe comedy - forced cheerfulness breaking down into practical reality. Great character moment for Moe.

jokes.text: Let me level with you, Marge. That's just our name for bottom-feeding suction eel.
jokes.punchline: Honest revelation about disgusting menu item
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of restaurant euphemisms. Moe's blunt honesty about terrible food is perfect character work.

jokes.text: I start with the best part- the neck. And then I add secret hobo spices.
jokes.punchline: Neck being the 'best part' and mysterious 'hobo spices'
jokes.explanation: Absurd food description that gets funnier the more you think about it. Perfect Moe-esque grossness.

jokes.text: What the hell are you doing, you little freak?
jokes.setup: Child pulling prank during family-friendly performance
jokes.punchline: Moe's immediate reversion to his true hostile nature
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe moment where Moe's facade completely collapses. The instant shift in tone is expertly handled.

jokes.text: I'm not used to the laughter of children. It cuts through me like a dentist's drill.
jokes.punchline: Revealing children's laughter physically pains him
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character insight disguised as an excuse. The dental drill comparison is perfectly specific and dark.

jokes.text: Silence, you fool. It can be ours.
jokes.punchline: Moe eyeing their leftover food like treasure
jokes.explanation: Perfect desperation comedy. Moe treating abandoning customers as opportunity rather than failure.

jokes.text: The one you call Milhouse... is gone.
jokes.punchline: Dramatic announcement that he's just visiting grandma
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection playing on Bart's panic. The formal phrasing makes the mundane sound ominous.

jokes.text: Oh, your 'teef' hurt, huh? Your 'teef' hurt? Well, that's too freakin' bad!
jokes.setup: Child complaining drink is too cold
jokes.punchline: Moe mocking child's pronunciation before losing it completely
jokes.explanation: Peak cringe comedy - Moe's complete inability to handle children. The mocking repetition before the explosion is perfect.

jokes.text: Whenever Uncle Moe threatens you, you get a free steak-fish.
jokes.punchline: Treating threats as a regular menu feature
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation that treats his violent nature as a business model. Great callback to the disgusting menu item.

jokes.text: Alfred Hitchcock stole every idea I ever had! He came into my room and-
jokes.punchline: Paranoid delusions about famous director
jokes.explanation: Standard 'crazy person' humor with specific celebrity reference. Serviceable but not exceptional.

jokes.text: A simple proposition, Ralph. How would you like to make a dollar? All you have to do is sign a paper that says I can have your soul.
jokes.punchline: Exploiting Ralph's innocence for soul transaction
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bart's desperation, though the setup is somewhat predictable.

jokes.text: Why? That was the problem in the first place. You were going broke because we were your only customers.
jokes.setup: Moe being comforted by his regular customers
jokes.punchline: Barney pointing out they're the source of his financial problems
jokes.explanation: Perfect logical observation that punctures the moment. Barney's rare moment of clarity is brilliantly timed.

jokes.text: Alf pogs! Remember Alf? He's back, in pog form!
jokes.setup: Milhouse explaining what he traded the soul for
jokes.punchline: Enthusiastic promotion of worthless collectibles referencing cancelled TV show
jokes.explanation: Iconic line that perfectly captures 90s collectible culture and Milhouse's naive enthusiasm. Became a cultural touchstone.

jokes.text: Since my breakfast burrito is congealing rapidly, I will be blunt.
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy talking to desperate Bart
jokes.punchline: Explains his impatience through gross food imagery
jokes.explanation: Perfect Comic Book Guy - pretentious language applied to disgusting fast food. 'Congealing rapidly' is wonderfully clinical.

jokes.text: But they were most interested in having possession of a little boy's soul.
jokes.punchline: Ominous implication about the buyer's intentions
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor that heightens the stakes. Comic Book Guy's clinical delivery makes it funnier and more sinister.

jokes.text: It contains a very rare Mary Worth... in which she has advised a friend to commit suicide, thank you.
jokes.setup: Telling Bart not to bang head on display case
jokes.punchline: Extremely dark content in wholesome comic strip
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of wholesome newspaper comic. The contrast between Mary Worth's usual content and suicide advice is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: There's no change in your piggy bank. Not in any of the ones you know about.
jokes.setup: Bart questioning how Lisa afforded to buy his soul back
jokes.punchline: Lisa revealing secret piggy bank stash
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa character moment showing her hidden depths and planning. The casual delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: But you know, Bart, some philosophers believe... that nobody is born with a soul... that you have to earn one through suffering and thought and prayer... like you did last night.
jokes.punchline: Lisa turning Bart's ordeal into philosophical lesson
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa character moment that provides both resolution and deeper meaning. Slightly verbose but earns its length.
